title: Watch this 100x developer use Codex… it’s insane
summary: David Ondrej features Pietro Schirano demonstrating the use of Codex for software development. The video showcases an exceptionally productive developer using AI code generation, with promotional content around coding with AI learning and hiring.
writeup: An interview with Petro, founder of Magic Path and former Anthropic employee, who walks through his live Codex workflow and argues that AI coding agents are collapsing the traditional role boundaries between engineers, designers, and PMs. The through-line is a concrete thesis: the browser is becoming a secondary surface, agents are becoming the primary interface, and the winning product strategy shifts from building destinations people visit to building context layers that agents can consume. The interview grounds these claims in a live mobile demo and a specific SDK prediction about where OpenAI is heading.
key_points: Petro abandoned Claude Code five months before this recording in favor of Codex, citing two concrete reasons: Codex has a tighter agentic loop with less bloat, and its token consumption for the same task is substantially lower, as confirmed by DeepSeek benchmark comparisons showing Codex outperforming Claude Code.

Magic Path provides agents like Codex with structured designer context at inference time, which Petro demonstrates produces better UI output than running the model cold, framing context injection as the key lever for front-end quality rather than model choice alone.

The core business-model argument is that the habit of going to a website to accomplish a task is dying: roles are collapsing (designer-engineer-PM boundaries are eroding), workers are choosing their own agent harness, and the only durable moats in this environment are brand and community rather than a locked-in workflow tool.

Petro predicts OpenAI will release a Codex SDK that lets browser applications surface events directly into the agent chat, making the browser a secondary surface the agent dips into rather than the primary environment the user lives in, a structural inversion of how products like ChatGPT Atlas were architected.

The failure mode of Atlas, per Petro, was being browser-first and agent-second: users found it faster to just open a browser themselves. Codex succeeds because it is agent-first, and browser interaction is an opt-in capability rather than the defining constraint.

The future-of-work thesis is that as models keep improving, the probability of a human manually adjusting individual details approaches zero, so the product opportunity is not to help people do the thing but to give them a platform for supervising the agent that does it.
